questionText: Disprove the statement: ``If the product $ab$ is even, then $a$ and $b$ are both even.''

steps.description: Read the claim as a conditional for all integers.
steps.workingLatex: \forall\ a,b:\ ab \text{ even} \Rightarrow a, b \text{ both even}
steps.explanation: The claim asserts an implication for *all* $a,b$; one pair with $ab$ even but not both even disproves it.
We need a single pair satisfying the hypothesis but failing the conclusion.

steps.description: Recall what makes a product even.
steps.workingLatex: ab \text{ even} \iff a \text{ even or } b \text{ even}
steps.explanation: Only *one* factor needs to be even, so we can keep the other odd — that breaks the `both even' conclusion.

steps.description: Choose one odd and one even.
steps.explanation: With $a=1$ (odd) and $b=2$ (even), the product will be even but $a$ is not.
The product $2$ is even, so the hypothesis holds.
But $a=1$ is odd, so `both even' is false.
With $a=1,\ b=2$ the product is even yet $a$ is odd, so the claim is disproved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Take $a=1,\ b=2$: then $ab = 2$ is even, but $a=1$ is odd. Hence the statement is false. $\blacksquare$
